description: The hard, rooted structures of the mouth used for biting and grinding food. Teeth are the body's smonaes—mineral, enduring, yet ultimately temporary. They enable the transformation of food into nourishment through patient grinding work, and their appearance marks life stages from infancy through elderhood.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 'k', the hard velar stop that mirrors teeth's firmness—a sound made in the back of the mouth near where molars grind. The 'u' adds depth and strength. Then 'la' introduces the liquid that softens, suggesting how teeth work with the flowing tongue. Finally 'i' rises bright and sharp like the edge of an incisor.
grammatical_notes: Typically used in plural contexts with 'sheloi' (many) or as collective. Single tooth specified with 'ta kulai' (one tooth). Milk teeth vs. adult teeth can be specified with modifiers.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Teeth carry the memory of what we've eaten, marking our relationship with the land's food. Their condition reveals life history to those who can read it.
pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Teeth remind us of embodiment and impermanence—they grow, decay, and fall, yet continue their work as long as they remain. The body's tools for transformation.
